covered_parties: Library-guide users seeking readings, organizations, networks, tools, and case studies about Indigenous data sovereignty.
license_or_terms: CC-licensed library-guide footer; specific linked works retain their own terms.
notes: No requirements were imported from linked materials that are not part of this content version.

requirements.none_reason: The saved libguide is a descriptive bibliography and resource index; it summarizes and links external works but imposes no partner-facing data-governance obligation of its own.

title: Indigenous Data Sovereignty — Native American Studies Library Guide

tsdf_mapping.notes: A bibliography does not itself establish an operational sharing rule.

covered_data: Knowledge and data created and owned by Tribes, including resilience actions derived from public plans, information selected for sharing, use and ownership terms, and information subject to opt-out and removal.
covered_parties: The Tribal Resilience Action Database project team and Tribal Nations, organizations, and communities whose publicly posted climate adaptation, resilience, or managed-retreat plans or derived resilience actions may be included in the database.
notes: The page describes the database project's policy approach and completed outreach; modal choices preserve 'aims,' definitional duties, 'will,' and the continuing opt-out permission without treating the page as enacted law.

claims.cite: Approach to Data Integration, Permissions, and Use ¶¶ 2-4
claims.claim: The database project aims to follow Free, Prior, and Informed Consent and Cause No Harm when potentially integrating and using knowledge and data created and owned by Tribes.
claims.conditions: The page describes these as the project's two guiding principles and does not present them as enacted law or an executed agreement.

claims.cite: Free, Prior, and Informed Consent paragraph
claims.claim: Under the project's stated FPIC approach, the project team is to ensure procedural and substantive fairness, involve Indigenous peoples from the beginning, and affirm their right to decline database engagement or participation at any time, now or later.
claims.conditions: This claim states the page's definition of how FPIC applies to this database project; it is not generalized to all projects or converted into enacted legal force.

claims.claim: Under the project's stated Cause No Harm approach, the project team will define partners' roles and responsibilities, the information to be shared, and the use, ownership, interpretation, and further-sharing terms at the outset, while honoring respect, trust, equity, and empowerment.
claims.conditions: Applies to the database project's own Cause No Harm approach, not as a universal legal rule.

claims.cite: Engagement process, final-letter and first-release paragraphs
claims.claim: A Tribe may opt out of the database at any time and have its data removed.
claims.conditions: The page describes this continuing option for Tribes whose public plans or derived resilience actions are included; it does not state a separate retention schedule or deletion method.

title: Data Sovereignty — Free, Prior, and Informed Consent

tsdf_mapping.notes: Advance agreement over use and sharing resembles T2 negotiation, while opt-out and removal go beyond TSDF tier behavior. Public availability is expressly not treated as sufficient consent: the project conducted outreach and offered withdrawal.

covered_data: Resolution titles and links across health topics, including the listed 2026:004 resolution on Tribal data sovereignty and intergovernmental data sharing.
covered_parties: Users of the USET SPF health-resolutions index and the organizations or officials identified only in listed resolution titles.
issuing_entity: United South & Eastern Tribes Sovereignty Protection Fund
notes: No operative language was inferred from the title 'Strengthening Tribal Data Sovereignty and Intergovernmental Data Sharing to Advance Tribal Public Health.' The linked resolution is outside this content version.

requirements.none_reason: The saved page is a resolutions index that supplies only the title and link for resolution 2026:004; it does not contain that resolution's recitals or operative directives.

title: Health Resolutions

tsdf_mapping.notes: A resolution title alone cannot establish a TSDF-relevant obligation or mapping.

covered_data: Indigenous data in any format, including information, specimens, and knowledges held by Indigenous Peoples or non-Indigenous entities that relate to, are about, or may affect Indigenous Peoples collectively or individually.
covered_parties: Individuals and entities involved in creating, collecting, assessing, analyzing, interpreting, managing, disseminating, or reusing data in the United States and its territories, including Tribal leaders, researchers, institutions, funders, governments, and other data actors.
notes: The brief reports a 2024 workshop and summit and contains normative calls and affirmations, but its legal status remains scholarship/descriptive rather than enacted law or an executed agreement.

claims.cite: Establish Indigenous Core Values for Data Futures (PDF p. 2)
claims.claim: Data actors must adhere to each Indigenous People's distinct values and ethical frameworks that guide that community's specific data-governance principles and strategies.
claims.conditions: The brief expressly requires recognition that principles and strategies are community-specific; it does not authorize substituting a generalized Indigenous standard for a particular People's framework.

claims.cite: Commitment to US Indigenous Peoples' Data Standard (PDF p. 3)
claims.claim: Indigenous Peoples' data standards must originate from and be governed by Indigenous Peoples.
claims.conditions: The statement concerns standards for defining, collecting, managing, storing, using, commercializing, representing, formatting, and transmitting Indigenous data across the lifecycle.

claims.cite: A Call To Action from Honorable Brian Vallo (PDF pp. 1-2)
claims.claim: Tribal leaders, researchers, institutions, funders, and governments are called on to work together to develop policies, procedures, and laws that uphold Indigenous Peoples' rights, responsibilities, and expectations in response to extractive research and misuse of Indigenous data and knowledge.
claims.conditions: This is the brief's reported call to action, not an enacted or universally binding rule.

title: Indigenous Data Governance Brief

tsdf_mapping.notes: TSDF's sovereign classification can support community-specific handling, but tier behavior does not establish Indigenous-origin standards, rights-based enforcement, provenance, governance authority, restorative justice, dynamic consent, or benefit and harm rules.

covered_data: Proposed project details, summary goals and outputs, developed or reused technology, semiannual progress updates, final reports, and any underlying Tribal environmental or program data that a Tribe elects to share or withhold.
covered_parties: Tribal applicants and recipients under the EPA Exchange Network Grant Program and EPA grant administrators.
notes: The FAQ's 'no current obligation' is recorded as descriptive program guidance, while the explicitly required closeout and reporting items remain mandatory and narrowly scoped.

claims.claim: The Exchange Network Grant Program currently imposes no obligation on a Tribe to share its actual data outside the Tribe.
claims.conditions: The FAQ distinguishes underlying data from required grant summaries and reports; other grant programs, such as Clean Water Act § 106 funding, may have different requirements.

claims.claim: At project closeout, a Tribal grant recipient must submit the IT Component and Project Registration form with summary information about accomplished goals and outputs and technology developed or reused, but need not share actual data or specific code.
claims.conditions: Applies to the Exchange Network Grant Program closeout form; the obligation is limited to summary information and does not extend to underlying data or specific code.

claims.claim: A Tribal applicant or recipient must provide project details in application documents, progress updates in semiannual reports, and a final report, while remaining free from an Exchange Network obligation to flow actual data outside the Tribe.
claims.conditions: The proposed project must support at least one Exchange Network funding area and meet the program's other requirements; the Tribe may explain its no-external-sharing policy in the application.

claims.claim: A Tribe that chooses or is otherwise required to share some information outside the Tribe may determine which data to share and which data to withhold from external disclosure.
claims.conditions: The FAQ presents this discretion even where some sharing occurs for another required grant program or because an exchange is mutually beneficial; it does not define limits imposed by those other programs.

title: Exchange Network FAQ — Tribal concerns about grant data-sharing requirements

tsdf_mapping.notes: The no-underlying-data rule aligns with sovereign non-egress, but the grant program's summary-reporting boundary and Tribe-selected field-level withholding are more specific than a TSDF tier alone.

covered_data: USGS information products, data and software, Tribal names and identifiers, project-progress information, Indigenous place names, and data shared by Tribes with USGS.
covered_parties: USGS authors preparing science information products, including data and software, that relate to Tribal activities, collaborating Tribes, Tribal lands, or Indigenous place names.
notes: This FAQ is nonbinding author guidance. Its 'should,' 'may,' and FOIA qualifications are preserved; it is not treated as the stronger Survey Manual directive cataloged separately as inst-018.

claims.cite: FAQ — When should USGS authors identify specific Tribes?
claims.claim: USGS authors should identify a specific Tribe only when the Tribe collaborated on the project, USGS research occurred on or over lands under its jurisdiction, or identifying it is fundamental to the reported science results.
claims.conditions: Authors should use federally recognized names whenever possible; rare references to non-federally recognized groups must avoid implying federal recognition.

claims.cite: FAQ — When should USGS authors identify specific Tribes?
claims.claim: If a partnering Tribe requests in writing that USGS use an abbreviated or alternate name in an information product, the author may do so if the alternate form is defined at first use.
claims.conditions: Limited to a written request from a partnering Tribe and conditioned on defining the form at its first occurrence.

claims.cite: FAQ — Do FSP data release requirements apply to data Tribes share with the USGS?
claims.claim: Data a Tribe shares with USGS are not subject to USGS Fundamental Science Practices data-release requirements, but may still be subject to FOIA; release discussions should occur early and clear collection and sharing decisions should be recorded in a data-management plan and agreement.
claims.conditions: USGS cannot promise in advance that records are exempt from FOIA, and employees cannot bind the United States contrary to agency policy.

title: Tribal-Related Guidance for USGS Authors

tsdf_mapping.notes: Early negotiated handling and release limits resemble T2/T3 controls, but TSDF does not represent naming rules, federal-records law, or the need to document the FOIA boundary in agreements.

covered_data: Information provided by Tribes or collected by USGS concerning Indigenous Knowledge, cultural resources, culturally significant sites and their locations or attributes, Tribe-owned data, sensitive Tribal data, FOIA-responsive records, science products, contracts, and data-sharing agreements.
covered_parties: USGS offices, employees, managers, principal investigators, contractors, collaborators, and non-federal partners engaging with federally recognized Tribes; specified provisions also apply to Alaska Native Corporations and Native Hawaiian Organizations subject to the chapter's stated distinctions.
notes: The directive was issued March 27, 2025, replaces the 2020 chapter, and is retained until superseded. References to ANCs and NHOs are not broadened beyond the chapter's explicit government-to-corporation and government-to-sovereign distinctions.

claims.claim: USGS will include Tribal perspectives, priorities, and input in science planning and decision-making processes that affect Tribes.
claims.conditions: This is a USGS guiding value limited to Bureau science planning and decisions that affect Tribes.

claims.claim: A Tribe may request in writing that information concerning its Indigenous Knowledge, cultural resources, or culturally significant site locations or attributes be labeled sensitive, whether the Tribe supplied the information or USGS collected it.
claims.conditions: The request must be written and must concern one of the stated categories.

claims.claim: USGS will seek to honor a Tribe's sensitive-data designation request to the extent law and congressional, presidential, or secretarial mandates allow and will notify the Tribe if the request cannot be honored in whole or part.
claims.conditions: The protection is expressly qualified by applicable law and higher-level mandates; the notification duty is triggered by inability to honor the request fully.

claims.claim: USGS must mark sensitive data to guard against accidental release, and sensitive Tribal data shared with USGS are exempt from Fundamental Science Practices data-release requirements.
claims.conditions: The FSP exemption does not eliminate possible disclosure under FOIA, as separately stated in the directive.

claims.claim: USGS will not include sensitive archaeological- or sacred-site data in its products when a Tribe identifies the site, unless the Tribe or a federal authority mandates release; if USGS learns of the site only after distribution, the program office or principal investigator will work with the affected entity to try to restrict or obscure the data when appropriate and feasible.
claims.conditions: The prepublication restriction has express Tribal- or federal-mandate exceptions; postdistribution action is qualified by 'try,' appropriateness, and feasibility and is coordinated with the Office of Tribal Relations.

claims.claim: USGS contracts must require contractors or non-federal partners to safeguard and prevent release of data a Tribe has identified as sensitive.
claims.conditions: Applies to data identified as sensitive by a Tribe and to USGS contracts with the covered external parties.

claims.claim: When USGS receives a FOIA request for sensitive Tribal data, the USGS FOIA Officer will review it and coordinate with the Tribe, the Office of Tribal Relations, and the DOI Solicitor to determine whether and how far a legal basis supports withholding; USGS employees must explain the Bureau's limited protection ability before collecting data.
claims.conditions: FOIA exemptions are determined case by case and cannot be promised in advance; a withholding decision may be overturned on appeal or in federal court.

claims.claim: USGS will not share with other federal agencies data owned by a Tribe, including Indigenous Knowledge received from a Tribally recognized Knowledge Holder, without the Tribe's express written permission.
claims.conditions: This is an exception to the directive's general permission for USGS to share collected information, including sensitive data, with another federal agency through a DOI Data Sharing Agreement.

claims.claim: If a Tribe asks USGS to restrict sharing specified data beyond the federal government, USGS will not share those data with a non-federal entity without the Tribe's express permission, even when that entity funded the data collection.
claims.conditions: Triggered by a Tribal restriction request concerning specified data; the rule applies to non-federal recipients, including funding partners.

title: Survey Manual 500.6 — Relations with American Indian and Alaska Native Tribes, Alaska Native Corporations, and Native Hawaiian Organizations

tsdf_mapping.notes: Sensitive labeling and permission-gated release align with sovereign tier controls, but TSDF does not encode FOIA uncertainty, case-by-case statutory exemptions, postpublication mitigation, Tribe-owned-data exceptions within federal sharing, contract clause mandates, or consultation duties.

covered_data: The resource page does not state the example agreement's data scope or terms.
covered_parties: A Tribal Nation and an institution or researcher as the contemplated parties to the linked example agreement; the saved resource page itself binds no party.
issuing_entity: Johns Hopkins Center for Indigenous Health
notes: The page says the example agreement between a Tribal Nation and an institution or researcher was drafted by CIRCLE team members and was added June 10, 2025. No terms were inferred from the linked PDF.

requirements.none_reason: The saved resource page only describes and links an example agreement; the downloadable template's clauses are not present in this content version and cannot be cataloged from the page.

title: Data Sharing Agreement — Resource Page

tsdf_mapping.notes: No operative template clauses are present to map.

covered_data: Policies, agreements, program implementation, consultation communications, agency activity reports, liaison contact information, training materials, and Office of Indian Affairs public reports concerning state-Tribal relations.
covered_parties: Washington state agencies, their heads, tribal liaisons and employees, the governor and statewide elected officials, and the Washington Office of Indian Affairs in relations with federally recognized Indian tribes whose traditional lands and territories included parts of Washington.
notes: Full-chapter replacement record. Claims capture government-to-government duties in the operative text, including provisions added or amended in 2026; they do not treat the chapter's no-private-right-of-action clause as weakening the stated agency duties.

claims.claim: Washington state agencies must make reasonable efforts to collaborate with covered Indian tribes when developing policies, agreements, and program implementation that directly affect those tribes and must develop an agency consultation process for issues involving specific tribes.
claims.conditions: The collaboration duty is limited to matters directly affecting covered tribes; additional offers of consultation apply to the actions and agencies specified in RCW 70A.02.100 and 70A.65.305.

claims.claim: Each Washington state agency must designate a tribal liaison who reports directly to the agency head.
claims.conditions: Applies to state agencies as defined in RCW 43.376.010(2).

claims.claim: A state-agency tribal liaison must help develop and implement communication and collaboration policies, serve as a contact with tribal governments, maintain communication with affected tribal governments, and coordinate employee training in government-to-government relations.
claims.conditions: These are the responsibilities of the designated liaison position within a state agency.

claims.claim: State agencies must ensure that tribal liaisons who interact with tribes and agency executive directors receive training covering effective state-Tribal communication and collaboration, cultural competency in services to tribal governments and members, and use of available training services.
claims.conditions: Applies to the training required by RCW 43.376.020(3) and its minimum content in RCW 43.376.040.

claims.claim: Each state agency must submit an annual report to the governor on its activities involving Indian tribes and its implementation of the chapter.
claims.conditions: Applies annually to each state agency covered by the chapter.

claims.claim: At least annually, the governor and other statewide elected officials must meet with leaders of covered Indian tribes to address issues of mutual concern.
claims.conditions: The meeting duty is limited to leaders of tribes within the chapter's definition.

claims.claim: The Washington Office of Indian Affairs must consult tribal nations when developing expanded state-employee training on government-to-government relations.
claims.conditions: Applies to the additional training produced with relevant state agencies and agency tribal liaisons under RCW 43.376.500(2)(c).

claims.claim: The Washington Office of Indian Affairs must regularly convene tribal nations and relevant state parties to discuss issues of mutual interest in a timely manner and must facilitate annual meetings with tribal leaders to implement the Centennial Accords.
claims.conditions: The annual meetings include agenda development, identification of joint strategies, and pursuit of specific goals.

title: RCW Chapter 43.376 — Government-to-Government Relationship with Indian Tribes

tsdf_mapping.notes: The chapter establishes government-to-government collaboration, consultation, liaison, training, meeting, and reporting duties rather than data-classification or egress behavior. These governance-process obligations require mechanisms outside TSDF tiers.

covered_data: Identifiable patient health-care information and records, especially adult and minor mental-health admission, treatment, prognosis, medication, behavior, commitment, discharge, location, public-safety, and care-coordination information shared with or by Tribal health and justice actors.
covered_parties: Washington health care providers and facilities, mental health service agencies, patients, Indian health care providers, tribal courts and prosecutors, tribal law-enforcement and public-health officers, and personnel of tribal jails, detention facilities, and other authorized recipients.
notes: Full-chapter replacement record. Per assignment, claims are deliberately limited to provisions touching Tribes, tribal health providers or public-health authorities, tribal courts and justice actors, or intergovernmental data sharing; unrelated medical-record minutiae are not exhaustively cataloged. The currently effective pre-July-2027 version of RCW 70.02.230 is cited where applicable.

claims.claim: For chapter 70.02, federal, state, or local law-enforcement authorities include empowered officers of a tribe or a tribal political subdivision, so the chapter's expressly conditioned law-enforcement disclosures extend to qualifying Tribal officers.
claims.conditions: A Tribal officer must be legally empowered to investigate an official inquiry into a potential crime or prosecute or conduct a criminal proceeding; each operative disclosure remains subject to its own conditions and data limits.

claims.claim: Adult mental-health information and records may be disclosed to tribal courts only as necessary to administer chapter 71.05 RCW or equivalent tribal-court proceedings, or to prevent an inconsistent evaluation or treatment order in the specified chapter 10.77 proceeding.
claims.conditions: The baseline rule is confidentiality and nondisclosure; this is a limited statutory exception, and the subsection characterizes the disclosure as mandatory for HIPAA purposes.

claims.claim: A prosecuting attorney, including a tribal prosecuting attorney, must be given access to a committed person's treatment, prognosis, medication, behavior-problem, and other relevant records needed to assess whether less restrictive treatment is in the person's or others' best interest.
claims.conditions: Access is limited to the prosecutor's responsibilities under RCW 71.05.330(2), 71.05.340(1)(b), and 71.05.335, and disclosure may occur only after notice to the committed person and counsel.

claims.claim: For a minor's mental-health records, tribal law-enforcement or tribal public-health officers may receive information necessary for their official responsibilities.
claims.conditions: This is one of an exclusive list of confidentiality exceptions and remains limited to necessity; the separate minimum disclosure required upon request is cataloged in c010.

claims.claim: When a minor escapes, disappears from treatment, violates less-restrictive-treatment conditions, or fails to return from leave, tribal law-enforcement and tribal public-health officers may receive only information necessary for public safety or apprehension.
claims.conditions: Disclosure is triggered by one of the listed events and limited to necessity; the recipients' separate confidentiality duty is cataloged in c011.

claims.claim: Upon a qualifying request, a mental health service agency must release specified commitment, discharge, address, and covered mental-health information to authorized recipients including personnel of a tribal jail or tribal detention or holding facility.
claims.conditions: The request must arise in the course of business and for official responsibilities; the expanded records in RCW 70.02.260(1)(a)(ii) are limited to specified custody, serious-violent-offense, or dismissed-charge circumstances, and privileged attorney material is excluded.

claims.claim: A person requesting the expanded mental-health records under RCW 70.02.260 must use an authorized official purpose, have the required reasonable suspicion, keep received information confidential, and redisclose it only through the section's narrow coordination, prosecutorial-advice, or statutory exceptions.
claims.conditions: The restrictions apply to requests under § 70.02.260(1)(a)(ii); information may be shared with similarly entitled persons only to coordinate official activities concerning the subject, or under the other express exceptions.

claims.claim: A request under RCW 70.02.260 does not require the record subject's consent but must be written, clearly identify the requester, and specify the requested information, except for the section's limited emergency oral-request procedure.
claims.conditions: An emergency oral request must later be confirmed in writing, and the immediate response is limited to treatment status and location or whereabouts information; federal substance-use-record restrictions may further limit release.

claims.claim: Tribal law-enforcement officers and tribal prosecutors enforcing Tribal laws or court orders comparable to the cited state firearms restriction may receive the specified confidential commitment information, and may release it only as allowed for the covered enforcement proceeding.
claims.conditions: The information is limited to commitment facts and orders and the notice of firearms ineligibility; the recipient must enforce a comparable Tribal law or order and remains within the subsection's release limits.

claims.claim: Upon a qualifying request by tribal law-enforcement or tribal public-health officers carrying out official responsibilities, only the fact and date of a minor's admission, date of discharge, treatment provider's name and address, and last known address must be disclosed.
claims.conditions: Applies to the request-based disclosure in RCW 70.02.240(7); the statute does not make broader disclosure mandatory under that paragraph.

claims.claim: Tribal law-enforcement and tribal public-health officers receiving minor mental-health information under the escape, disappearance, treatment-violation, or unauthorized-leave exception must keep the information confidential under chapter 70.02 RCW.
claims.conditions: Applies to information disclosed under RCW 70.02.240(8), which is itself limited to what is necessary for public safety or apprehension.

title: RCW Chapter 70.02 — Medical Records — Health Care Information Access and Disclosure

tsdf_mapping.notes: TSDF can support confidential handling and restrictive sharing at a high level, but it does not encode role-specific statutory entitlements, event triggers, minimum-necessary fields, notice, reasonable-suspicion predicates, comparable-Tribal-law conditions, or HIPAA characterization.

covered_data: Financial-assistance records, financial statements, audits, program reports, contract and self-governance records, staffing and funding reports, health-status and service-delivery data, funding-agreement information, and Tribal comments on federal reports.
covered_parties: The Secretaries of the Interior and Health and Human Services, the Comptroller General, recipients of federal financial assistance, Indian tribes, Tribal organizations, and contractors operating under Indian self-determination or self-governance agreements.
notes: Corrective replacement for the wrong-content finding in bl-028, which remains unchanged as an audit-trail record. Claims are deliberately scoped to data, records, reporting, information access, and consultation provisions relevant to Tribal data sharing; the chapter's broader contracting minutiae are not exhaustively cataloged.

claims.claim: Recipients of federal financial assistance under the chapter must keep prescribed records fully disclosing the amount and disposition of assistance, project cost, nonfederal cost share, and audit-facilitating information; mature-contract records are limited to specified financial statements, an annual single-agency audit, and a brief annual program report.
claims.conditions: The general records are as prescribed by regulation; the narrower mature-contract list applies when the contract has mature status under § 5304(h).

claims.claim: The Comptroller General and the appropriate Secretary must have access for audit and examination to recipient books, documents, papers, and records related and pertinent to chapter assistance.
claims.conditions: Access lasts until three years after the end of the regulatory retention period for the submitted report and is limited to materials related and pertinent to the grants or contracts.

claims.claim: A recipient of federal financial assistance under the chapter must make reports and information available to the Indian people it serves or represents.
claims.conditions: Availability is as and in the manner the Secretary determines adequate.

claims.claim: The appropriate Secretary must report annually in writing to each tribe on projected and actual staffing levels, funding obligations, and expenditures for programs operated directly by the Secretary that serve that tribe.
claims.conditions: Applies to programs directly operated by the Secretary that serve the receiving tribe.

claims.claim: A Tribal organization expending at least $500,000 in federal awards in a fiscal year must submit a single-agency audit; any additional program information is limited to what the Tribal organization may negotiate with the Secretary, and reporting disputes follow the statutory declination criteria and procedures.
claims.conditions: The audit threshold and fiscal-year condition apply; additional reporting is negotiated rather than unilaterally required.

claims.claim: Under the model self-determination agreement, contractor records generally are not federal records; the contractor must maintain a records system and provide reasonable access upon reasonable advance request.
claims.conditions: Previously provided copies may be treated as federal records only when the Secretary demonstrates they are clearly required for Department of the Interior or HHS recordkeeping; monitoring visits are limited to two per year unless the contractor agrees to more or the Secretary gives reasonable-cause advance notice describing the problem.

claims.claim: The Secretary must prepare and supply relevant information to a self-determination contractor and promptly comply with requests for information the contractor reasonably needs to determine the successor annual funding amount.
claims.conditions: The requested information must be reasonably needed for the successor annual funding agreement.

claims.claim: Unless a tribe specifies otherwise in a Department of the Interior compact or funding agreement, Tribal records are not federal records; the tribe must maintain a records system and provide the Secretary reasonable access after at least 30 days' notice.
claims.conditions: Access is only sufficient to enable the Department to meet its minimum legal recordkeeping obligations under title 44.

claims.claim: Before submitting the annual Department of the Interior self-governance report to Congress, the Secretary must distribute it to tribes for at least 30 days of comment and include tribes' separate views and comments; specified program determinations and revisions require consultation with tribes.
claims.conditions: The report is compiled from funding agreements, audits, and Secretary data; consultation duties apply to the program determinations and annual revised lists specified in the section.

claims.claim: Unless a tribe specifies otherwise in an HHS compact or funding agreement, Tribal records are not federal records; the tribe must maintain a records system and provide the Secretary reasonable access after 30 days' advance notice.
claims.conditions: Access is only sufficient to enable HHS to meet its minimum legal recordkeeping requirements.

claims.claim: HHS compacts or funding agreements must require a tribe to report health-status and service-delivery data only to the extent that the data are not otherwise available to the Secretary, specific funds are provided for the reporting, burdens are minimal, and the requirements are promulgated under § 5397.
claims.conditions: All four statutory limitations constrain the permissible reporting requirement.

claims.claim: The Secretary's annual HHS self-governance report may not impose reporting requirements beyond those otherwise provided in the chapter and must be distributed to tribes for at least 30 days of comment, with tribes' separate views and comments included.
claims.conditions: The report is compiled from funding agreements, audits, and Secretary data; the related report on the funding method is prepared after consultation with tribes.

title: 25 U.S.C. Chapter 46 — Indian Self-Determination and Education Assistance

tsdf_mapping.notes: TSDF can help keep contractor and Tribal records under restrictive controls, but it does not encode statutory audit access, negotiated reporting, minimum-recordkeeping access, funding predicates, report-and-comment procedure, or the chapter's federal-record status rules.

covered_data: Policies with tribal implications, Tribal input and consultation communications, tribal summary impact statements, written agency-Tribal communications, waiver applications and decisions, and agency certifications to the Office of Management and Budget.
covered_parties: Executive departments and agencies other than independent regulatory agencies; independent regulatory agencies are encouraged to comply. The order concerns federally recognized Indian tribal governments.
notes: Corrective replacement for the empty wrong-content finding in bl-030, which remains unchanged as an audit-trail record. This record catalogs only EO 13175 from the full Federal Register text. Its operative agency duties are recorded as mandatory where the order says 'shall,' while independent-agency compliance and consensual mechanisms retain their nonmandatory wording; § 10 makes the order internally managerial and non-judicially enforceable.

claims.claim: When formulating and implementing policies with tribal implications, agencies must follow the order's federalism, self-government, trust, treaty, and government-to-government principles.
claims.conditions: Applies to regulations, legislative comments or proposed legislation, and other policy statements or actions having the order's specified substantial direct effects.

claims.claim: To the extent permitted by law, agencies must respect Tribal self-government and sovereignty, honor treaty and other rights, and grant tribal governments maximum administrative discretion when administering federal statutes and regulations.
claims.conditions: The duties apply to the extent permitted by law; maximum discretion concerns federal statutes and regulations administered by tribal governments.

claims.claim: When undertaking to formulate and implement policies with tribal implications, agencies must encourage tribes to develop their own policies, must where possible defer to tribal standards, and must consult tribes on the need for federal standards and alternatives that would limit their scope or preserve tribal prerogatives and authority.
claims.conditions: Deferral is qualified by 'where possible'; consultation concerns federal standards under development.

claims.claim: Each agency must establish an accountable process ensuring meaningful and timely Tribal input in developing regulatory policies with tribal implications.
claims.conditions: Applies to regulatory policies with tribal implications; the order separately defines both 'agency' and 'policies that have tribal implications.'

claims.claim: An agency may not issue a regulation with tribal implications that imposes substantial direct compliance costs on tribal governments and is not required by statute unless the federal government funds those costs or the agency completes the order's early-consultation and tribal-summary-impact process.
claims.conditions: The prohibition and alternatives apply only to the extent practicable and permitted by law; under the consultation alternative, the agency must consult early, submit a tribal summary impact statement to OMB, and make written Tribal communications available to OMB.

claims.claim: An agency may not issue a regulation that has tribal implications and preempts tribal law unless it completes the order's early-consultation and tribal-summary-impact process.
claims.conditions: The prohibition applies only to the extent practicable and permitted by law; the process requires early consultation, an OMB tribal summary impact statement, and availability to OMB of written Tribal communications.

claims.claim: When appropriate, agencies should explore and, where appropriate, use consensual mechanisms for developing regulations on issues relating to Tribal self-government, trust resources, or treaty and other rights.
claims.conditions: Both exploration and use are expressly conditioned on appropriateness.

claims.claim: Agencies must review their waiver processes, streamline applications, and, to the extent practicable and permitted by law, consider tribal applications to waive statutory or regulatory requirements; a complete application must be decided within 120 days or another legally required period, and a denial must receive timely written reasons.
claims.conditions: Only discretionary and waivable requirements are covered; consideration is qualified by practicability and law, and the decision period yields to another period established by law.

claims.claim: Agencies must provide OMB designated certifications for draft final regulations and proposed legislation that have tribal implications.
claims.conditions: The regulation certification addresses compliance with § 5; the legislation certification states consistency with § 4 or the need for an exception.

claims.claim: Independent regulatory agencies are encouraged to comply with the order.
claims.conditions: Independent regulatory agencies are excluded from the order's definition of agency and therefore are encouraged rather than bound.

claims.claim: EO 13175 is intended only to improve internal executive-branch management and does not create a judicially enforceable right or benefit or diminish federal trust responsibilities.
claims.conditions: This limitation applies to enforcement by a party against the United States, its agencies or instrumentalities, officers or employees, or any other person.

title: Executive Order 13175 — Consultation and Coordination With Indian Tribal Governments

tsdf_mapping.notes: The order governs consultation, policy development, waiver process, impact statements, and executive oversight rather than artifact classification or egress. Those government-to-government process controls require mechanisms outside TSDF tiers.

covered_data: Tribal, state, and federal public-health data; record-level PII and PHI; the Northwest Tribal Registry; Tribal Data Hub data; Tribe- and Tribal-area-specific aggregates; analytic files, transfers, reports, publications, media releases, and destruction records.
covered_parties: Northwest Tribal Epidemiology Center and NPAIHB staff, project directors, data stewards and custodians, contractors, consultants, interns, external collaborators, data requesters, member Tribes, and data providers, but only as each handbook provision specifies.
notes: DRAFT machine extraction of an intertribal handbook, not a Nation enactment. Correction ev-director-0141 governs the manifest's conservative nation-authored mislabel. Formal adoption or internal enforceability is not established by the retrieved bytes, so legal status remains nonbinding guidance.

claims.claim: NPAIHB employees requesting Tribal data for an NPAIHB project must make the request in writing to the Tribal council, state the proposed use and users and how confidentiality will be maintained, include a sample resolution or data-sharing agreement authorizing release, and obtain the NPAIHB Executive Director's approval.
claims.conditions: Applies to employee requests for Tribal data needed for NPAIHB projects; the request contents and executive approval are quoted from the handbook's incorporated NPAIHB policy.

claims.claim: Records obtained for NPAIHB projects that contain personal or Tribe-specific information must be protected from uses other than those for which they were collected, limited to personnel assigned to the project, and not disclosed without prior written consent from the individual or Tribal government.
claims.conditions: Applies to project records containing personal or Tribe-specific information; consent is attributed to the affected individual or Tribal government as applicable.

claims.claim: Access to record-level PII and PHI must be limited to the minimum number of people who need it for their job functions, with the appropriate level of access determined by the designated NWTEC leadership.
claims.conditions: Applies to record-level PII and PHI; the handbook separately assigns authorization for the Registry and Data Hub to their project leadership and procedures.

claims.cite: Training and Documentation Requirements; Table 1 (PDF pp. 11-14)
claims.claim: NWTEC staff, interns, contractors, external collaborators, and other people with access to managed PII or PHI must complete the applicable security, confidentiality, and documentation requirements.
claims.conditions: Asset-specific requirements vary. Some short-term or limited-scope contractors and interns may be exempt from some requirements, but at minimum must complete the NWTEC confidentiality pledge and IT-required security training; specified asset classes state no exceptions.

claims.claim: Contractors, consultants, and interns are expected to follow internal and external policies for protecting data and maintaining confidentiality.
claims.conditions: Applies to contractors, consultants, and interns, including people whose short-term or limited-scope engagement may alter separate training and documentation requirements.

claims.claim: Users accessing NPAIHB network-server data must use an NPAIHB-issued computer, connect on site or through the NPAIHB VPN, authenticate with a unique user ID and a password meeting the stated complexity and rotation rules, and use multi-factor authentication in the stated circumstances.
claims.conditions: Applies to information stored on NPAIHB network servers; multi-factor authentication is required for Office 365, a new device, or a password change.

claims.cite: Storage of PHI and Tribal Data on NPAIHB's servers (PDF p. 15)
claims.claim: Record-level PHI from any source and record-level or aggregated Tribal data must be stored in restricted-access server locations available only to designated people, with folder access approved by the NWTEC Director.
claims.conditions: Applies to NPAIHB server storage of the identified PHI and Tribal data; the handbook notes that IT creates the restricted locations and updates access lists on request.

claims.cite: Storage of PHI and Tribal Data on individual computers (PDF pp. 15-16)
claims.claim: An analyst storing a necessary local copy of PHI or Tribal data must first ensure the computer uses the specified validated AES-256 encryption and authenticated login, and the user must securely shred the data after analysis is complete.
claims.conditions: Local storage is not advised and is allowed only where necessary; the encryption and destruction requirements apply before local download and after analysis, respectively.

claims.cite: Storage and Use of Personally Identifiable Information/NTR files (PDF p. 16)
claims.claim: Use of the Northwest Tribal Registry must follow the handbook's layered safeguards: the linkage device remains off public networks during linkages, is secured when not in use, stays with staff during travel, uses secure shredding for confidential files, maintains only encrypted backup storage, and is accessible only to the minimum approved and trained staff.
claims.conditions: Applies specifically to the Registry file, linkage device, and encrypted backup described by the handbook.

claims.claim: An appropriate agreement, such as a completed data-sharing agreement, must be in place before a data transfer occurs.
claims.conditions: The handbook states this as the general transfer rule; later sections describe a limited exception for returning certain aggregated state or federal data to a requesting Tribe and distinguish when particular Tribal agreements and addenda are required.

claims.claim: NWTEC staff should use agency-operated secure portals or secure file-transfer sites for state and federal transfers and should use the identified secure SharePoint process for confidential transfers with Tribes or other partners.
claims.conditions: The first method applies where state or federal providers make their secure services available; SharePoint is described as the preferred method for confidential Tribal and other partner datasets.

claims.cite: When should data be destroyed? (PDF p. 17)
claims.claim: Retention, return, and destruction of data must follow the governing provider agreement; for project-specific Tribal data without such guidance, NWTEC must ask the Tribe at project end for its destruction preference and permanently destroy copies left on electronic transfer platforms after confirming collaborators have the required project files and outputs.
claims.conditions: Active agreements may permit retention of ongoing surveillance data. For project-specific Tribal data, the DSA controls; the direct Tribal follow-up applies only when no destruction or return guidance is available.

claims.cite: Protocol for reporting a data breach or loss (PDF p. 18)
claims.claim: For a known or suspected loss of sensitive information, NWTEC must follow the internal escalation process and notify data owners, DSA contacts, or co-investigators in writing within one week.
claims.conditions: The one-week notice applies unless a signed DSA states otherwise or public-safety personnel or the IRB directs otherwise; additional reporting applies to stolen devices, linkage data, and IRB research.

claims.cite: Release of Tribe- or Tribal Area-Specific Data (PDF p. 24)
claims.claim: Data about an individual Tribe or Tribal service area may be released only with permission from the authorizing official identified in that Tribe's DSA.
claims.conditions: Applies to Tribe- or Tribal-area-specific data and the authorizing official or alternate specified in the Tribe's DSA.

claims.cite: Release of Tribe- or Tribal Area-Specific Data (PDF p. 24)
claims.claim: Under no circumstances should Tribe-specific data be released to an entity outside that Tribe without the Tribe's written permission.
claims.conditions: Applies to entities outside the Tribe; the handbook uses 'should' even with the 'under no circumstances' qualifier.

claims.cite: Release of Tribe- or Tribal Area-Specific Data (PDF p. 24)
claims.claim: Staff must release Tribe-specific data only to people employed by the Tribe who are authorized to receive it.
claims.conditions: Staff must avoid inadvertent release to IHS clinic personnel, contractors, consultants, or unauthorized recipients included in multi-recipient communications unless the Tribe has provided the required permission.

claims.cite: Release of Tribe- or Tribal Area-Specific Data (PDF p. 24)
claims.claim: A release about a sub-state region encompassing multiple Tribes may require written permission from those Tribes or a data-sharing agreement.
claims.conditions: Applies to data for sub-state geographies that encompass multiple Tribes; the handbook does not make permission categorically mandatory for every such region.

claims.cite: Release of Tribe- or Tribal Area-Specific Data (PDF p. 24)
claims.claim: Staff must consult their supervisor or the NWTEC Director to determine the appropriate approach for releasing data about a sub-state region encompassing multiple Tribes.
claims.conditions: Applies to proposed releases about multi-Tribe sub-state regions and does not itself decide whether permission or a DSA is required in a particular case.

claims.cite: Release of Tribe- or Tribal Area-Specific Data (PDF pp. 24-25)
claims.claim: When an outside entity requests Tribe-specific data, NWTEC may respond by having the Tribe request and then release the data, having the outside entity seek the Tribe's written authorization for NWTEC release, or establishing a multiparty data-sharing agreement among the entity, Tribe, and NWTEC.
claims.conditions: The available option depends on staff capacity and the requesting circumstances; the handbook presents these as response options rather than guaranteed access.

claims.cite: Data Sharing Agreements with Tribes (PDF p. 25)
claims.claim: A Tribe must have a DSA and Data Hub addendum to access the Northwest Tribal Data Hub, and a DSA and General Data Exchange addendum are required for exchange of Tribally collected data between a Tribe and NWTEC.
claims.conditions: A DSA is preferred but not currently required for data-request technical-assistance responses; aggregated state and federal data may be returned to Tribes without a DSA only when released under the handbook's Tribe-specific release guidance.

claims.cite: Small Numbers and Data Suppression Guidance (PDF p. 25)
claims.claim: NWTEC reporting should suppress nonzero counts below five and rates or proportions calculated from such counts.
claims.conditions: This is stated as the handbook's general suppression guideline; state and federal systems may impose higher thresholds and additional system-specific restrictions that take precedence.

claims.cite: Small Numbers and Data Suppression Guidance (PDF p. 26)
claims.claim: When presenting data, analysts must ensure suppressed cells cannot be recalculated by aggregating data or applying secondary suppression as necessary.
claims.conditions: Applies to presentations containing suppressed cells; aggregation and secondary suppression are the stated methods for preventing reconstruction.

claims.cite: Small Numbers and Data Suppression Guidance, provider table (PDF pp. 26-29)
claims.claim: Analysts must follow the handbook's data-provider-specific release restrictions, including applicable higher suppression thresholds, prohibitions on identifiable or line-level releases, permission requirements for identified institutions, and internal-use-only limitations.
claims.conditions: The exact restriction depends on the named state or federal data system; the table contains system-specific exceptions and does not create a single threshold for all datasets.

claims.cite: Review Requirements for Peer-Reviewed Publications and Conference Presentations and other Data Releases (PDF p. 29)
claims.claim: A peer-reviewed publication with an NWTEC staff co-author must be reviewed and approved by the Portland Area IHS IRB before publication.
claims.conditions: Applies to peer-reviewed publications that include NWTEC staff as co-authors.

claims.cite: Review Requirements for Peer-Reviewed Publications and Conference Presentations and other Data Releases (PDF p. 30)
claims.claim: A conference presentation reporting analytic findings should be submitted to the Portland Area IHS IRB before presentation.
claims.conditions: The handbook permits submission after the abstract has been officially accepted and provides a project-protocol route for presentations using data stewarded by the identified NWTEC units.

claims.claim: Publications and other products using covered provider data must satisfy the applicable provider-specific review, approval, advance-notice, attribution, and final-copy requirements summarized by the handbook.
claims.conditions: Requirements and deadlines vary by data system. The RHINO review rule exempts urgent Tribal requests supporting immediate public-health action from prerelease program review but still requires copies of developed products as stated in the table.

claims.claim: Authors of peer-reviewed manuscripts and conference abstracts should invite qualifying project leadership and staff involved in data acquisition, quality improvement, and preparation to be co-authors, and should acknowledge Northwest Tribes or NPAIHB delegates when possible.
claims.conditions: The co-author invitation remains subject to the handbook's authorship criteria and review requirements; acknowledgement is qualified by 'when possible.'

claims.cite: Release of data to the media and through social media channels (PDF p. 32)
claims.claim: Media data responses must follow the handbook and NPAIHB media policy; employees must not speak for NPAIHB without express executive approval, and aggregated social-media data releases must follow the handbook and social-media guidance and receive executive approval before release.
claims.conditions: Applies to media inquiries and aggregated data released through social-media channels; inquiries are directed to the institutional executive role.

claims.cite: Appendix A, limited datasets with record-level health information (PDF pp. 36-37)
claims.claim: For limited datasets containing record-level health information, access must be least-privilege and time-limited, record-level data must not be re-released to nonstaff or contractors without provider approval, storage and transfer must use the stated encryption controls, and datasets must remain segregated or otherwise distinguishable for return, destruction, and breach assessment.
claims.conditions: These are general appendix requirements for state and federal limited datasets; the applicable DSA may add system-specific requirements.

claims.claim: Aggregated Tribe- or Tribal-area-specific data may be provided only to authorized people within the Tribe or people the Tribe designates, and must not be released at a level that could identify an individual.
claims.conditions: Applies to aggregated data released through products, the Data Hub, or other means and incorporates the handbook's suppression guidelines.

Provider-specific consent, benefit-sharing, review bodies, purpose limits, incident deadlines, and conditional exceptions are not fully represented by tier assignment.

covered_data: Data and samples collected from or about Tribal populations; Tribal attributions and inferences; human-subject, biological, genetic, genomic, environmental, cultural, and Indigenous Knowledge materials; secondary analyses, publications, transfer, reuse, and disposal.
covered_parties: NIH-funded researchers and studies, Tribal Nations and communities, individual participants, Tribal review bodies, and data stewards discussed in the presentation's proposed Indigenous Data Sovereignty policy takeaways; the slides do not establish a final NIH policy.
issuing_entity: National Institutes of Health Tribal Health Research Office
notes: DRAFT machine extraction of a presentation describing a policy-development process, proposed takeaways, and open questions. It is not represented as a final NIH Indigenous Data Sovereignty policy. Flag-scoped content was omitted under owner ruling ev-director-0183.

claims.claim: The presentation's policy-development takeaways state that a Tribe holds authority over data collected on Tribal lands or data from which inferences or attributions to that Tribe can be made, and that ownership and authority over Tribal data rest with the Tribe.
claims.conditions: Presented as a policy-development takeaway rather than an adopted NIH rule.

claims.claim: Under the presentation's proposed approach, data management, sharing, access, reuse, transfer, and disposal must receive Tribal approval before data collection.
claims.conditions: Applies to Tribal data within the presentation's IDS policy-development scope; the slides are draft policy material.

claims.claim: Future transfer, de-identification, and return plans must be established, and open or unrestricted access must not be the default; a Tribe must affirmatively opt in with clear plans and approvals.
claims.conditions: Presented as a policy-development takeaway for covered Tribal data and future use, not as a final NIH policy.

claims.claim: Individual and collective benefits, including dissemination, publication strategies, and benefit sharing, should be stated in consent materials and data plans in plain language.
claims.conditions: The presentation addresses both the Tribe and individual participants and identifies possible agreements as a vehicle for mutual understanding.

claims.claim: Research planners must clarify with the Tribe in writing who owns or co-owns the data, who will steward it, how and where it will be stewarded, and what cultural protections apply to the data and participating communities.
claims.conditions: The slide gives this as an imperative within draft policy-development takeaways and before collection planning is finalized.

claims.cite: Tribal review and approval takeaways (PDF p. 10)
claims.claim: The presentation states that a Tribe has the right to designate the Tribal or non-Tribal IRB or other review body and the single- or multiple-IRB approval process, and that final approval to proceed remains with the Tribe, including any retained secondary review.
claims.conditions: The process and any retained secondary review are to be determined before data collection; Tribal assent alone is not treated as consent.

claims.cite: Tribal review and approval takeaways (PDF p. 10)
claims.claim: A process for obtaining formal Tribal review and approval should be established before data collection through the Tribal council or another review body designated by the Tribe.
claims.conditions: The Tribe determines the appropriate approval body; examples in the presentation are illustrative rather than exhaustive.

claims.cite: Tribal review and approval takeaways (PDF p. 10)
claims.claim: When relevant, cultural-stewardship protocols for living and nonliving data should be identified before data collection.
claims.conditions: Applies where the data or samples implicate cultural stewardship; the presentation proposes treating covered data and samples with protections comparable to human-subject protections.

claims.cite: Individual rights and disposal takeaways (PDF p. 10)
claims.claim: A Tribe must follow an individual participant's consent concerning data disposal and must not override that consent.
claims.conditions: Applies where individual consent specifies disposal; the slide states that Tribal authority does not override the individual's disposal choice.

claims.cite: Individual rights and disposal takeaways (PDF p. 10)
claims.claim: Legacy and dynamic re-consent plans must receive Tribal approval before data collection, and consent planning for data associated with deceased people must identify disposition, transfer, disposal, and who will decide.
claims.conditions: The slide addresses longitudinal and deceased-person data; it recommends identifying a re-consent timeframe if disposal is not planned.

claims.cite: Biological samples and Tribal consent takeaways (PDF p. 11)
claims.claim: Tribal consent and re-consent must be obtained for analyses or secondary analyses that may identify a Tribe or create impacts, inferences, or implications for that Tribe.
claims.conditions: Triggered by Tribal identification, inference, implication, or impact rather than by every off-reservation analysis.

claims.cite: Biological samples and Tribal consent takeaways (PDF p. 11)
claims.claim: The presentation recommends ending irreversible and timeless consent for biological, genetic, genomic, and derived materials.
claims.conditions: Presented as a proposed IDS practice rather than an adopted prohibition.

claims.cite: Biological samples and Tribal consent takeaways (PDF p. 11)
claims.claim: Clear biological-sample consent options and consent or re-consent plans must be identified at the outset.
claims.conditions: Applies to the sample categories covered by the slide and is presented as a policy-development need.

claims.cite: Points of Tension: urban/off-reservation sample (PDF p. 12)
claims.claim: A study may proceed without Tribal approval when it makes no inference to a Tribal community, is not collected on Tribal land or at an IHS urban facility, and involves an off-reservation participant.
claims.conditions: Every stated condition must be satisfied; the slide separately raises overlap with nearby Tribal lands as requiring additional caution.

claims.cite: Points of Tension: urban/off-reservation sample (PDF p. 12)
claims.claim: For an urban sample near or overlapping Tribal lands, consultation with nearby Tribes and an agreement preventing Tribe-specific analysis without Tribal approval are recommended as best practice.
claims.conditions: The recommendation supplements the page's off-reservation criteria and applies to the stated geographic-overlap scenario.

claims.claim: Planned or secondary analyses that will make inferences to a specific Tribe or Tribal community must obtain Tribal consent and formal approval under that Tribe's laws or protocols before conducting the study or analysis or publishing or otherwise disseminating findings.
claims.conditions: Triggered where Tribe-specific inference can affect the social, cultural, economic, or general welfare of the identified Nation, village, or community.

claims.cite: Points of Consideration: sample repatriation (PDF p. 22)
claims.claim: The proposed approach describes honoring a Tribe's request to repatriate samples or transfer them to a designated repository unless doing so conflicts with the original individual's consent.
claims.conditions: The slide gives individual consent to destruction as the example of a controlling conflict.

title: THRO Overview: Indigenous Data Sovereignty

tsdf_mapping.notes: Tribal approval before collection and reuse, opt-in access, restricted secondary analysis, and repatriation resemble negotiated T2 and sovereign T3 controls. Collective consent, Tribal IRB designation, benefit-sharing, cultural guardianship, re-consent, inference-based scope, and individual-versus-collective disposal rules are not fully represented by TSDF tiers.

covered_data: Tribal and AI/AN public-health data, including data held in state, federal, and other datasets, aggregated Tribal data, COVID-19 surveillance and immunization information, and other public-health systems addressed by the model provisions.
covered_parties: Tribal Nations, state and federal public-health jurisdictions, and parties considering the Washington health data-sharing agreement provisions excerpted by the presentation; the slides do not themselves execute or enact those provisions.
issuing_entity: American Indian Health Commission for Washington State
notes: DRAFT machine extraction of a nonbinding presentation. The Bureau of Justice Assistance hosts the file; the presentation identifies the American Indian Health Commission for Washington State as the presenting organization. Quoted agreement clauses are not represented as independently executed by the slides. Flag-scoped content was omitted under owner ruling ev-director-0184.

claims.claim: The presentation states as a core principle that only a Tribe has sovereign authority to determine how its data may or may not be used.
claims.conditions: Presented as a nonbinding data-sovereignty principle rather than as an enacted provision.

claims.claim: A jurisdiction reporting on or about American Indian or Alaska Native peoples should meaningfully partner and consult with Tribal leaders on analysis and interpretation of the data.
claims.conditions: Applies when a jurisdiction reports on or about the identified peoples; the slide attributes this as a core principle.

claims.claim: The presentation states that Tribes retain an ownership interest in Tribal data when the data are held in state, federal, or other datasets and when the data are aggregated with other data.
claims.conditions: Presented as a nonbinding core principle; the slide does not reproduce a governing ownership instrument for every dataset.

claims.claim: The presentation states that Tribes must have equitable access to data needed to perform their governmental duties.
claims.conditions: Presented as a core sovereignty principle rather than a self-executing access grant to any named system.

claims.cite: Tribal Data Sharing Agreements: Key Provisions (PDF p. 17)
claims.claim: The presentation describes a sovereignty-aligned Tribal data-sharing agreement as including government-to-government Tribal input on state and federal data use, state protection of Tribal and AI/AN data, recognition of Tribal ownership, and more equitable Tribal access for governmental duties.
claims.conditions: This is guidance on key agreement provisions; the slide does not state that every agreement already contains them.

covered_data: All BIA data assets collected, created, or acquired; metadata, information-system schemas and dictionaries, public and nonpublic data, controlled and personally identifiable information, geospatial data, open-government data assets, and data supplied to AI systems.
covered_parties: All programs and offices under Bureau of Indian Affairs authority, including BIA leadership, deputy bureau directors, the associate chief data officer, data stewards, data asset custodians and producers, system owners, and employees, only within each role's assigned responsibilities.
notes: Machine extraction treats the issued Indian Affairs Manual chapter as current internal agency policy effective May 13, 2024. The BIA Director approved the directive. Flag-scoped content was omitted under owner ruling ev-director-0186.

claims.claim: All BIA programs and offices must comply with applicable data regulations, requirements, and standards and promote sustainable data-management practices supporting the BIA mission.
claims.conditions: Applies throughout the BIA under the policy's stated scope; particular duties remain assigned by role in § 1.5.

claims.claim: The BIA Director must provide resources and organizational support for the Data Governance Program, coordinate policy and guidance with stakeholders, delegate data-management authority, make program objectives and policies clear, and ensure employees are aware of and trained on data policies and guidance.
claims.conditions: Applies to the institutional BIA Director role as Senior Accountable Official for the program.

claims.claim: The identified deputy bureau directors must oversee and periodically review data governance in their areas, provide policy and technical assistance, and assign data stewards and data asset custodians.
claims.conditions: Applies within each deputy director's services, functions, offices, or regions.

claims.claim: The BIA associate chief data officer must implement applicable data directives, policies, and guidelines; maintain data-asset inventories; monitor internal controls; protect data confidentiality, integrity, and availability; and support openness, accuracy, and completeness.
claims.conditions: Applies to data assets collected, created, or acquired under applicable statute, regulation, and departmental policy, with support from the other roles identified in the directive.

claims.claim: A BIA data steward must manage assigned data assets, ensure compliance with departmental directives and data-category, metadata, and quality-control requirements, ensure custodial documentation and maintenance, and complete required data-governance training.
claims.conditions: Applies to data assets within the steward's assigned area of responsibility.

claims.claim: A BIA data asset custodian must manage assigned assets for storage, integrity, and security, make public assets available in a compliant machine-readable format, support implementation of asset requirements, and complete required training.
claims.conditions: Applies within the custodian's technical-management area; public-availability duty is limited to public data assets and departmental standards.

claims.claim: A BIA data asset producer must create or acquire data digitally, create and manage metadata under departmental requirements, and implement quality control for BIA data management.
claims.conditions: Applies to the producer role when it creates, collects, acquires, or distributes data.

claims.claim: A BIA system owner must document every data asset within the system, keep the associate chief data officer, steward, and custodian informed of those assets, and maintain system schemas and data dictionaries.
claims.conditions: Applies to information systems for which the role procures, develops, integrates, modifies, operates, maintains, or disposes.

claims.claim: BIA employees must not disclose nonpublic data, including through a prompt, to a generative-AI system unless the Department has authorized that system for use.
claims.conditions: The prohibition applies only to nonpublic data and lasts unless and until the Department authorizes the specific generative-AI system.

claims.claim: All existing Departmental policies must continue to be followed when BIA uses or procures AI technologies.
claims.conditions: Applies to AI use and procurement within the Department; the directive does not replace more specific policies.

claims.claim: BIA must implement internal controls ensuring that metadata records are created and managed under Department Data Governance Board directives and federal regulations.
claims.conditions: Applies to metadata records governed by the continuing standards in § 1.7.

claims.claim: BIA personnel must follow the Department's open-data inventory policy and make covered data assets open-government data assets by default unless law or regulation restricts doing so.
claims.conditions: Applies to assets collected, created, or acquired after enactment of the OPEN Government Data Act; the open default is expressly limited by legal or regulatory restrictions.

claims.claim: A person must complete the directive's specified minimum courses or authorized equivalents before serving as a BIA data steward or data asset custodian.
claims.conditions: Applies before service in either identified role; authorized equivalent training satisfies the requirement.

claims.claim: After completing the designated courses, data stewards and data asset custodians should notify the BIA associate chief data officer for inclusion in the trained-workforce database.
claims.conditions: Applies as the designated courses are completed and is stated as a recommendation rather than a prerequisite.
